Assessing Sustainability Efforts and Greenwashing 
in Global Oil and Gas Supply Chain- Natural 
Language Processing Approach 
 
Sanchari Ghosh  
Department of Management Studies 
and Industrial Engineering 
Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), 
Dhanbad 
Jharkhand, India 
22dr0240@iitism.ac.in  
    
Sandeep Mondal 
Department of Management Studies 
and Industrial Engineering 
Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), 
Dhanbad 
Jharkhand, India 
sandeepmondal@iitism.ac.in  
Abstract— Global oil and gas supply chain ensures energy 
security of the world; however, faces significant compliance 
pressure on environmental footprints and allegation of 
overwhelming in sustainability reporting from the society. This 
paper proposes a natural language processing based unified 
framework to evaluate the environmental compliances as per 
global standards and greenwashing detection. It extracts 
keywords and numeric data from the sustainability reports, 
analyses sentiments for the entire supply chain operations, and 
matches with the pre trained global standard thresholds as per 
the United Nation Climate Action Declaration 2022 to classify 
the compliances and mark the probable greenwashing. Thus, 
the study offers a valuable tool for stakeholders and regulators 
to assess accuracy and transparency in corporate sustainability 
practices. 
Keywords— 
Sustainability 
Compliance, 
Greenwashing 
Detection, Natural Language Processing 
I. 
INTRODUCTION 
The global oil and gas supply chain (OGSC) plays a vital 
role in fuelling the world economy yet faces intense scrutiny 
due to its environmental impacts, with the increasing concerns 
about climate change and natural resources depletion, various 
governments worldwide have now mandated the publication 
of environment social and governance (ESG) reports 
periodically. Thus, the companies are increasingly compelled 
to publish the ESG reports annually to showcase their 
commitment towards sustainability. However, scepticisms are 
rising on the transparency and accuracy of the reports with the 
allegations of greenwashing where companies exaggerate and 
misrepresent their ESG efforts [1]-[3]. 
Scrutinising the ESG reports to assess the credibility and 
corporate adherence towards sustainability goals poses 
significant challenges. Traditional analysis might be 
subjective as well as labour intensive too, thus creates a need 
of efficient scalable time bound and objective solution. 
Machine learning (ML) stands here as a promising alternative 
by automating the evaluation process of such extensive data 
into meaningful summaries with limited subjectivity and least 
time [4]. 
ML techniques such as natural language processing (NLP) 
has emerged as popular method for automating the analysis of 
large unstructured text data expediently. NLP is able to 
facilitate in extracting essential metrics in the reports, 
recognising patterns in corporate language, and thus 
highlights discrepancies between stated claims and actual 
performance. Techniques such as tokenization, named entity 
recognition (NER), sentiment analysis, etc. enable the 
processing of large volumes of text, reveal subtle signs of 
greenwashing and evaluate the credibility of sustainability 
commitments [4]-[6]. 
The objective of this paper is to build a framework that 
leverages NLP to assess the sustainability efforts for OGSC 
by evaluating environmental compliances against established 
benchmarks proposed under United Nation Sustainability 
Development Goals (UNSDGs) and detect greenwashing as 
per UN Climate Action Declaration 2022. 
Next sections elaborate related works, followed by 
methodology, implementation framework, data analysis and 
result, and conclusion, respectively. 
II. 
RELATED WORKS 
Researchers have developed models to monitor corporate 
adherence to environmental regulations and standards, 
including compliance with the UNSDGs. These models 
include unsupervised learning frameworks that automatically 
classify companies based on their compliance with 
environmental regulations by extracting features from 
sustainability reports [7]. A study on applied deep learning 
develops a model to assess the environmental compliance of 
companies operating in the energy sector [8]. However, their 
work did not focus on unstructured textual data from 
sustainability reports, which can provide deeper insights into 
corporate intentions and practices. Greenwashing detection in 
sustainability reports has recently become a rising concern as 
more organizations are engaging in sustainability reporting 
practices [1]-[2]. Models build on applying NLP and deep 
learning techniques leverages sector-specific compliance 
indicators and ESGs [9]. Sentiment analysis is a method used 
in assessing the tone of reports and advertisements and 
detecting over-optimistic or neutral languages [10]. 
Combining sentiment analysis with sustainability indicators 
provides a more comprehensive assessment on such 
company's claims. Machine learning models trained on 
labelled 
data 
have 
proven 
promising 
in 
detecting 
greenwashing [11]. Previous studies suggest for creating 
benchmarks on greenwashing detection [12]. Some ML 
models show unsupervised learning methods on unstructured

ESG data for better generalisation across industries [13]-[15]. 
Another set of studies explores the usages of ML algorithms 
in optimising supply chain operations while ensuring 
environmental sustainability. Such work also emphasizes how 
the ML models could predict supply chain disruptions and 
also recommends sustainable operation strategies [16]-[17]. 
Thus, existing literatures lack in a targeted focus on using 
NLP in assessing compliances across this oil and gas sector. 
So, this study tries to develop a framework to evaluate 
sustainability compliance and greenwashing detection 
specifically for the OGSC. 
III. 
METHODOLOGY 
The proposed framework has been designed to process 
reports by extracting certain pre-defined features related to 
environmental compliances and greenwashing. OGSC 
broadly distributed in three segments: extraction of crude oil 
from the borewells, i.e., Upstream segment; storage and 
transportation of crude to refineries under Midstream 
segment; and finally refining crude into final products and 
distributions in consumer market, i.e., Downstream segment 
[18]. To assess the complete chain, 5 leading companies under 
each stream, i.e., a total 15 companies are selected whose ESG 
reports for the year 2023 are considered for further analysis. 
A. Feature Selection 
Feature Selection is performed to reduce the input, ignore 
the noise and pick only relevant datasets for the analysis. 
Sustainability efforts will be different under various supply 
chain operations. Greenwashing actions can be traced by 
figuring out statements like claiming to be on track to reduce 
emissions, being vague about SDG target achievements, using 
misleading labels, emphasising on environmental attributes, 
or communicating sustainability attributes in isolation. Table 
1 demonstrates such features on the OGSC sustainability 
efforts and greenwashing detection stream-wise.

IV. 
IMPLEMENTATION FRAMEWORK 
The following algorithms are used to analysis the 
framework. 
A. Algorithm 1: Data Extraction and Preprocessing 
 
It extracts textual data from ESG reports and 
prepares the texts according to module 1 and 2, respectively. 
Input: ESG reports in .pdf format 
1. Load the ESG report using PDF parsing tools. 
2. Extract raw text from the report. 
3. Preprocess the text: 
a. 
Convert text to lowercase. 
b. Remove special characters and digits 
using regular expressions. 
c. 
Tokenise the text into individual words. 
d. Remove stop-words from the tokenized 
text. 
e. 
Apply lemmatization to reduce words to 
their base form. 
4. Store the cleaned and pre-processed text for 
analysis. 
Output: Pre-processed text data ready for NLP analysis. 
B. Algorithm 2: Sentiment Analysis 
This algorithm performs sentiment analysis for tone 
identification of sustainability claims and flag potential 
greenwashing statements. 
Input: Pre-processed text data from ESG report 
1. Load a pre-trained sentiment analysis model. 
2. For each sustainability-related claim in the text: 
a. 
Analyse the sentiment of the text 
b. Classify the sentiment 
3. Flag potential greenwashing: 
a. 
If sentiment is overly positive without 
supporting 
data, 
flag 
as 
"Possible 
Greenwashing." 
b. If the sentiment is neutral or negative with 
evidence, classify as "Legitimate Claim." 
4. Store sentiment classifications and greenwashing 
flags. 
 
Output: 
Sentiment 
classifications 
and 
flagged 
greenwashing instances. 
C. Algorithm 3: Sustainability Assessment (Environmental 
Compliance) 
These algorithms assess sustainability efforts across 
various segments of OGSC. 
 
a)  Algorithm 3.1: Upstream Sustainability Assessment 
(Environmental Compliance) 
Input: Cleaned text data from ESG report for upstream 
1. Extract sustainability metrics relevant to upstream 
operations: 
a. 
Emissions data (UNSDG 13) 
b. Water usage (UNSDG 6) 
2. Compare 
extracted 
metrics 
with 
predefined 
thresholds for compliance: 
a. 
Emissions reduction > X% → Compliant 
b. Water usage reduction targets > Y% → 
Compliant 
c. 
If reductions are between X% and (X-Δ) 
% for emissions or Y% and (Y-Δ) % for 
water usage → Partially compliant 
d. Else → Non-compliant 
3. Classify environmental compliance based on 
results. 
Output: 
Sustainability 
compliance 
classification 
(Compliant, Non-compliant, Partially compliant) 
 
b) Algorithm 3.2:Midstream Sustainability Assessment 
Input: Cleaned text data from ESG reports 
1. Extract sustainability metrics relevant to the 
midstream operations: 
a. 
Energy 
efficiency 
in 
transportation 
(aligned UNSDG 7) 
b. Pipeline safety and leak prevention 
(UNSDG 12) 
2. Compare extracted metrics with predefined 
thresholds: 
a. 
Energy efficiency improvement > X% → 
Compliant 
b. Pipeline safety incident <Y→ Compliant 
c. 
If improvements are between X% and 
(X-Δ) % for energy efficiency or safety 
incidents are only slightly above Y% → 
Partially compliant 
d. Else → Non-compliant 
3. Classify environmental compliance. 
Output: 
Sustainability 
compliance 
classification 
(Compliant, Non-compliant, Partially compliant). 
 
c) Algorithm 
3.3: 
Downstream 
Sustainability 
Assessment (Environmental Compliance): 
Input: Cleaned text data from ESG reports for 
downstream 
1. Extract 
sustainability 
metrics 
relevant 
to 
downstream operations: 
a. 
Refinery emissions and waste management 
(UNSDG 13) 
b. Renewable energy adoption in refining 
(UNSDG 7) 
2. Compare 
extracted 
metrics 
with 
predefined 
thresholds: 
a. 
Emission reductions > X% → Compliant 
b. Renewable energy usage > Y→ Compliant 
c. 
If reductions are between X% and (X-Δ) 
% for emissions or renewable energy usage 
is slightly below Y% →Partially compliant 
d. Else → non-compliant 
3. Classify environmental compliance. 
Output: Sustainability compliance classification 
(Compliant, Non-compliant, Partially compliant) 
D. Algorithm 4: Greenwashing Detection 
These algorithms assess greenwashing detection across 
various segments of OGSC.

4:
a) Algorithm 4.1: Upstream Greenwashing Detection: 
Input: ESG report for upstream company 
1. Identify vague or overly positive claims related to: 
a. 
Emissions reduction (without supporting 
data) 
b. Water management (without concrete 
figures) 
2. Use sentiment score and keyword matching to flag 
potential greenwashing: 
a. 
Positive sentiment with vague language → 
Flag as greenwashing 
b. Neutral 
or 
negative 
sentiment
→ 
Legitimate claims 
Output: Greenwashing flags in the report 
 
b) Algorithm 4.2: Midstream Greenwashing Detection: 
Input: ESG reports for midstream 
1. Identify exaggerated claims related to: 
a. 
Low emissions in transportation 
b. Use of renewable energy in midstream 
operations 
2. Use sentiment score and keyword analysis to detect 
unsupported claims: 
a. 
Positive sentiment without specific metrics 
→ Flag as greenwashing 
b. Neutral 
or 
negative 
sentiment 
→ 
Legitimate claims 
Output: Greenwashing flags in the report 
 
c) Algorithm 
4.3: 
Downstream 
Greenwashing 
Detection: 
Input: ESG reports for downstream  
1. Identify potentially misleading claims related to: 
a. 
"Eco-friendly" 
marketing 
in 
product 
labelling 
b. Energy mix claims without quantifiable 
data 
2. Use sentiment analysis and keyword matching to 
detect unsupported claims: 
a. 
Positive sentiment without data → Flag as 
greenwashing 
b. Neutral 
or 
negative 
sentiment 
→ 
Legitimate claims 
Output: Greenwashing flags in the report. 
 
 
This process has been run for each company based 
on its profile as upstream, midstream or downstream. Thus, a 
total of 15 company profiles are built by using this framework 
to defend the OGSC. All such relevant data and reports are 
sourced from publicly available company websites and UN 
sustainability data repositories, which are openly available 
for all with full accesses of reading and downloads. 
V. 
DATA ANALYSIS AND RESULT 
The analysis of 15 such prominent OGSC companies (stream-
wise) has been performed in Python programming in Google 
Colab platform. Various python libraries and packages are 
used to build this framework. Some of the major tools are 
pdfminer, used to extract raw text from the .pdf extension 
reports; nltk (Natural Language Toolkit) to pre-process texts 
by tokenizing, stop-word removal, and lemmatization; re 
(Regular Expressions) for cleaning special characters, 
numbers, and unnecessary symbols; TextBlob for sentiment 
analysis; NumPy, pandas, matplotlib, seaborn and scikit-
learn together used for efficient data analysis, visualization 
and error analysis purposes. Table 3 shows the analyses of the 
study in details. Predicted labels are marked by using this 
proposed framework and true labels are the original claims 
made by the reputed international auditing firms and policy 
regulators throughout the world on those published reports.

Above result highlights overall robustness of the 
framework, it has the best performance in predicting EC 
values for midstream and GW at downstream followed by 
EC, GW of the OGSC; EC of downstream; and EC, GW of 
the upstream, respectively. Figure 2 represents comparison of 
such performance metrics across different supply chain 
stages by combining EC and GW together to showcase 
overall robustness of the proposed framework.
